sentences.text: Not that I recall.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I was growing up, well, particularly in Santa Barbara, most of the-- of the fellas I palled around with were Italian, like Vince [ITALIAN], I didn't mention, and Dick [ITALIAN].
They were-- they were Italian.
I don't recall any Jewish families.
I'm sure there were some, but in those days, I didn't know Italian from Chinese, hardly.
[LAUGHS] I was color blind.
Everybody was just another person, and if he was my friend, he was my friend.
I didn't much care what his nationality was.
Same way.
Yeah.
I-- I don't think I ever-- in fact, I'm not now, am I-- do I have really any prejudices, except for bad actors, but there's bad actors in any nationality.
But I don't recall any particular [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] thoughts one way or another about a person's background, or their nationality, or anything.
We have a-- I have a very good Jewish friend in our church.
As a matter of fact, this is rather unique.
She's-- she's Jewish and-- and has Jewish faith, but she attends our church, Methodist Church, and she's very active in it.
She's a delightful person, sings in the choir.
And our minister, former minister, was black.
Just a beautiful, beautiful person, he and his wife.
And he was elevated recently to a District Superintendent in San Francisco.
So he's got 56 churches under his direction now.
And I think I'm very non-prejudiced.
I'm sure they did.
Because among the teachers that they were associated with, I'm sure there were some Jewish folks.
But again, I didn't attach any significance to-- to them as Jewish, or Italian, or Greek, or whatever.
And I'm sure they did, because they were associated with lots, lots of the faculty of Santa Barbara High School.
'33, I would've been nine years old.
Not-- I don't think so.
Not-- not then.
As a matter of fact, really, it was later on when-- when trouble started occurring in Europe.
Well, particularly in like, '36, '37, when I recognized the fact that there was trouble brewing in Europe.
Well, at the time, I-- I presumed that there was a power struggle going on in Europe.
Germany was kind of on one side, and France and Britain were on the other side, and we were, kind of the Americans, looking on and seeing what was going to happen.
And I didn't-- I didn't attach a whole lot of significance to it then.
I sure did later.
[LAUGHS] But it just didn't-- for instance, I didn't know that Dachau was-- the concentration camp established in 1933, was the first camp.
And I found that out a lot later, of course.
I didn't--
I think it must have been-- when I really attached some significance was the situation in Czechoslovakia.
And-- no.
That wasn't the first, was it?
Yeah, it was.
When the British prime minister said, all is going to be peaceful, ha ha.
And they march into Czechoslovakia, then next, it's Austria, then next it's France.
And I guess it was-- it was-- I guess the first was the-- the trouble that brewed in Germany itself.
The burning of the books and the Reichstag and all of that is when I first became, started becoming more and more aware of the trouble that was going on in Germany.
And then, of course, from the invasion of Czechoslovakia and the takeover of Austria.
Newspapers and radio, yes.
And of course, we discussed it in school.
And obviously in 1941, when I was still in school, then there-- the war was on.
Not really.
I wasn't-- I wasn't very conscious of the United States having to go to war until, of course, we did.
But I-- I just-- I lived my young life in Santa Barbara, and it was-- the war was in the background, really, in Europe.
And I didn't think in terms of the United States ever getting involved in it, other than being more or less friends of the Allied Britain and France.
And that there was this-- this bad thing going on in Germany with Hitler and his cronies.
But I didn't attach a lot of significance to that.
I attached a lot when the Japanese invaded Pearl Harbor, of course.
Then that was really-- really brought me to the realization that, oh boy, here we go.
I don't recall really, up until maybe 1939, really talking about it much.
I was too involved in my own pursuits as a youngster, as a teenager.
I didn't-- we didn't really talk about it.
It was-- it was happening, of course, but I don't really remember being affected very much by-- by that aspect of the conflict.
Yeah.
Well, he was doing his thing too, teaching ROTC.
And he didn't really get involved in World War II until the Japanese invasion.
And then, of course, he got his commission, and then he became a-- he was an umpire in Fort Lewis, Washington, when they had the War Games there in the late '41.
And then, of course, I got involved, and with my friends.
And I was in the ROTC, and we talked a lot about what was going on, and will the United States enter this war?
And sure enough, in '41, we entered it whole hog.
[CHUCKLES] Do you remember Pearl Harbor?
Do you remember that day?
Yes.
I remember.
[SIGHS] It was, of course, in '41.
December 7.
I was-- I was still in Santa Barbara.
And I recall hearing it on the radio.
And of course, I remember the-- the newspaper people coming down our street, calling, extra, extra, Japanese attack Pearl Harbor!
And of course, we were on the radio then, listening to what was going on, and-- and oh my goodness.

And then shortly thereafter, we had this Japanese submarine fired rounds-- came to the surface and fired rounds on the-- the beach in Santa Barbara and up near the oil fields in Goleta, that this Japanese sub had done that.
And of course, that involved us a lot, and we had the anti-aircraft guns going off, shooting at nothing.
[CHUCKLES] There was a lot of hysteria in-- shortly after the Pearl Harbor episode.
Well, I started realizing that young folks were-- were leaving and going to war.
And I began to wonder, well, I'm in a-- a military organization, the ROTC, in Santa Barbara High.
And am I going to be drafted when I become of age, or am I going to volunteer?
A lot of folks were going into the military.
And I said, well, maybe-- maybe that's not going to be too far off when I am.
Because I was certainly not against going in to-- to the war effort.
He was-- as I said, he was involved with having gotten his commission.
He was still involved in ROTC.
And then in-- in '42, as I said, moved to Van Nuys, or Sherman Oaks, and I went to Van Nuys high school in '41 or '42. '41.
September of '41, graduated in '42.
He was involved in that and not specifically in any war effort.
But he was a professor of military science and tactics at three high schools in the Los Angeles area; Hollywood High School, Fremont High School, and Van Nuys.
And-- and I think I told you the story of my experience with him at Van Nuys, because he was the boss there.
He-- when he was an umpire, during the maneuvers up in Washington, he had to go up there, and he was gone for a significant period of time.
As a matter of fact, when he was there, we went to-- my mother and sister and I went to Portland and stayed with my grandmother.
And so he was in Fort Lewis.
I was-- when I went to UCLA, I was in the ROTC at UCLA.
And our whole unit was called in.
So it was sort of being drafted and sort of enlisted, because I was involved in ROTC.
So it was kind of-- kind of a given that our unit was going to be taken into basic training as a unit.
And we did.
We went to Camp Roberts, California, north of Santa Barbara and south of Moterey, here.
And I spent my basic training months there.
So that was in May of '43, having been inducted, actually, at Fort MacArthur in San Pedro.
So I spent from May until September, I guess it was, in basic training, and then I stayed on as a cadre man at Camp Roberts.
I got promoted to colonel-- or corporal.
A little early for colonel-- corporal.
[LAUGHS]
And stayed there until [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] October, the end of October.
We were called back, all of us.
We were called back to UCLA to await opening of a class at either Fort Sill, Oklahoma or Fort Benning, Georgia to go through training to get our commissions.
So I went back to UCLA, and we lived in a fraternity house on Gayley.

[CHUCKLES] And then in the January of '44, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we were all called, sent back, to OCS, a couple of the OCS at us Fort Sill and Artillery and Infantry School at Fort Benning, Georgia.
And I got my commission then as a second lieutenant after spending two sessions in the hospital, one for measles, of all things.
[LAUGHS] And the second time, I had the flu.
So I had to drop out of two classes.
The second class I was in, as a matter of fact, I was a bunk mate with an All-American football player from Notre Dame whose name was Bill Shakespeare.
He was a punter and a full back for Notre Dame.
And of course, I was pretty enthralled with him.
And he was a wonderful guy, and we bunked next to one another.
And I finally graduated with this class in-- in August of '44 and then went on to assignment with the 42nd Rainbow Division in Camp Gruber, Oklahoma.
I was assigned to a company, a rifle company, in the 42nd Division, actually in the 222nd Infantry Regiment.
Colonel Luongo was our regimental commander, and I was in the Second Battalion at the time, under then, Major Donnard, Don Donnard.
And I was reassigned then to a company of the 222nd, which was in the First Battalion.
And the battalion commander was Walter Fellons, and he was a tiger, [CHUCKLES] He-- he was really a disciplinarian.
Colonel Donnard was a great officer, but he wasn't quite the disciplinarian that Fellons was.
But he was a-- he was a West Pointer and ramrod straight.
And I remember one day, we had a strange kind of salute to a superior officer.
We'd have to raise our chins and then salute.
And I didn't get my chin high enough one day, and Fellon says, Lieutenant, come here.
I said, yes sir.
How do you salute in the 42nd Division?
Well sir, I raise my chin, salute.
That's better, he says.
[LAUGHS]
As you were.
And so I spent-- we spent from August of-- actually September, because I went home for a delay in route.
From early September until November training.
I was a platoon leader.
In fact, I led the 3rd Platoon of A Company.

I remember one occasion, we were doing these combat exercises where the platoon would be marching along, and all a sudden, these targets would pop up, and we were supposed to deploy and take these targets under fire.
And this voice behind me said, Lieutenant, those targets are pretty hard to see, aren't they?
And I looked around.
It was a deputy division commander.
It was a brigadier general.
And I said, you know, yes sir.
And he said, well, why the hell don't you use the binoculars that you've got strapped to your back?
[CHUCKLES] Yes sir.
[CHUCKLES]
And so we packed and shipped over.
Packed in early November and we left New York Harbor in November of '44.
As a matter of fact, I took a contingent of troops aboard this big troop ship in New York Harbor from Camp Kilmer, which was our processing center then.
Aboard this troop ship, I had 20 or so folks.
And we got our area of the ship readied for the rest of the troops from A Company or from the battalion to come aboard, and I got seasick sitting in the harbor.

[LAUGHTER] But that was a blessing in disguise because we were on the high seas on Thanksgiving Day, and I was all over my sickness, and everybody else was sick, so we had food galore for Thanksgiving dinner.
This was-- ship was called the Americas.
It had been a German luxury liner, and it had been either sold or commandeered by-- I guess it was sold to the United States, and became a troop ship.
It was called The Americas.
It was quite a big ship.
When I went to the 42nd.
Well, I was-- I was sure that I'd be in combat when I went to Fort Benning.
The whole idea was to-- to get a commission and join a unit.
That was the whole idea.
And of course when I went to Oklahoma, I knew that it was only a matter of time that we'd be going-- I didn't know then whether we'd be going to the South Pacific or to Europe.
And I felt very fortunate that we went to Europe, because we are fighting an enemy that we understood more than we understood the Japanese.
So I-- I was pretty sure I'd be going, in-- in 40-- early '42, I was sure of it.
Because I was going to OCS for-- eventually, to go into combat.
Apprehensive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I knew that-- that folks were being killed.
And, of course, I-- I certainly wanted to survive to an old age.
But I think we mentioned this earlier, I-- I have a lot of faith.
I had a lot of faith then.
And I figured, I'm going to make it through.
I-- I I'm pretty-- pretty smart, militarily, and I know-- know how to use a weapon.
And I am, I think, a leader, and I know how to lead men.
I've-- I felt comfortable in that regard.
And so I-- I obviously apprehensive and a bit afraid, as I think everybody was who faced combat.
And I didn't want to-- didn't want to look at the Statue of Liberty when we shipped out because I thought, you know, some weeks hence, we're going to go into combat.
And I just don't-- I felt, I guess, a lonesomeness of homesickness maybe, leaving our country and going to a strange places that I'd never seen before.
How am I going to react when I get there?
Am I going to be [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so afraid that I'll be a coward when I get there?
And I didn't think I would, because I-- I-- I have a lot of-- of self confidence, or had then, I think, from various aspects of my civilian and my military background.
Did you feel any resentment that you have to go fight for--
No.
None at all.
No.

I was really fighting for the United States, although, of course, we were over there to help France and the British and the Dutch and everybody else, that we're fighting for them, but we're also fighting for the United States, and we're fighting against the Japanese directly.
We're fighting them because they attacked us.
But we're over there because there's a job to be done.
There's a-- there's a monster trying to take over the world in Europe, and he just about almost did it in Hitler and his folks.
And so I-- I felt no resentment at all.
I felt that's-- that's my duty.
That's my job.
I've gotta go through with it, as much as I-- there was a lot of-- a lot of trauma associated with-- with going into combat.
Yes.
Yes.
I said, well, as you know, I'm-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was right after I came home after OCS.

I said that we're-- we're going in to-- I'm going to be assigned to this division in Oklahoma, 42nd Division, and before I go-- of course, I never got to see her beyond that time, because we were so involved in Oklahoma, and so I didn't come home again.
But I did call her and talk to her on the phone and tell her that we were going overseas.
And I couldn't say a whole lot, because the-- the censors would have gotten upset with me if I had said a whole lot about where we were going or what-- where-- what sector of Europe we were going to.
I couldn't tell her that.
But I did tell her I loved her, and-- and I'll be back.
And I corresponded with her all the time.
Mhm.
Yes.
Well, again, he was very militarily oriented, and he felt very proud that I was going into combat, particularly since I was an officer and had a responsibility.
He-- he was, I think, pretty proud.
And so he felt that, yeah, this is something that you're going to do, and I'll miss you, but Godspeed, and-- and we'll see later.
[CHUCKLES]
No, no women at all.
It was all-- all male.
We had several units on this-- on this ship.
I think there-- our whole regiment, I believe, was on the ship that went over.
About 2,000, I believe.
Yeah, It was packed.
[CHUCKLES]
As I mentioned, I didn't-- I got sick in the harbor, so I was home free.
During the trip, I didn't get sick again.
And a lot of the folks were, because we were-- we were-- right after Thanksgiving, we ran into a storm.
And, of course, we were dodging U boats, submarines, German subs, all the way across.
So we had to go in a zigzag path and it took us a long time, from Thanksgiving until the middle of December, to get there.
So we were on seas three weeks.
And that was a long, long trip.
Well, there was-- there was a lot of reading.
I-- I had a-- a state room with another officer.
And I would go down and visit my troops, and we would-- we would talk about what was going to happen when we got into combat, and--
OK.
